examples.text: وَلَا يُرِيدُ أَحَدٌ أَهْلَ الْمَدِينَةِ بِسُوءٍ إِلَّا أَذَابَهُ اللَّهُ فِي النَّارِ ذَوْبَ الرَّصَاصِ أَوْ ذَوْبَ الْمِلْحِ فِي الْمَاءِ
examples.english: None should nurse ill-will towards the people of Medina, or God will melt him in fire like tin or like salt is in water.

examples.text: اَلْأُسْرُبُ يَتَوَلَّدُ كَٱلرَّصَاصُ وَهُوَ صِنْفٌ رَدِئٌ مِنْهُ
examples.english: Lead comes about like tin but is a worse kind thereof.

senses.gloss: stones heaped together to surround a grave or well

etymology.text: According to Arabic lexicographic tradition, derived from رَصَّ (raṣṣa, “to stick firmly, to join compactly, to make even or level, to file, to pile, to close”), “due to the interjoining of its parts”, but the metal meaning has been identified by Scher with Persian ارزیز (arzīz, “tin”), which for formal reasons and chronology would have to be specified as a semantic loan from Parthian *arčīč, cognate to Middle Armenian արճիճ (arčič, “lead”), though Fraenkel contended that “to stick firmly” could have led to the metal meaning via the meaning “to solder”.
